coredata.dc:title: X-ray absorption spectroscopy examination of Cr, Co, and Cu binding on fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Moonsuang, Budsaya
Thaitong, Benjamaporn
Amonpattaratkit, Penphitcha
Praingam, Ngamjit
Prayongpan, Pornpimol
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from tartaric acid and triethylenetetramine showed potential for the detection of Cr6+, Co2+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solution, with the most effective sensing being based on fluorescence quenching for Co2+. A good linear relationship between the concentration of Co2+ and the relative fluorescent intensity was obtained at 1.7–167.7 μM (R2 = 0.9993) with a limit of detection at 0.94 μM. X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) was used to examine the absorbed transition metal ions on the CDs. Measurements were carried out at Beamline 8, Synchrotron Light Research Institute, Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand. Cr, Co, and Cu K-edges were measured in various standard compounds containing different oxidation states. An energy shift of 12.49 eV in the absorption of the Co K-edge was observed from elemental Co0 to Co2+, 3+, while shifts of 17.96 eV and 5.45 eV were observed for elemental Cr0 to Cr6+ and elemental Cu0 to Cu2+, respectively. A small decrease in edge energy of the Cr K-edge XAS spectra compared to standard compounds with the LCF results suggested two forms of Cr6+ found in Cr sorption on carbon dots while a mix of Cu1+ and Cu2+ was found in Cu sorption on carbon dots. Interestingly, an increase in edge energy of Co K-edge was observed for Co sorption on carbon dots compared to +2 oxidation state compounds, suggesting that metal species transform to a higher positive charge during binding to CDs. Cobalt species might participate in the formation of a chemical bond with functional groups existing on the surface of CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra confirmed the results from XAS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
X-ray absorption spectroscopy
Synchrotron
Energy shift
Metal ions

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special section on "Asian Conference on X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy 2019"

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: K-edge XANES spectra of different Cr standard compounds and Cr sorption on carbon dots (K2Cr2O7/CDs).
K-edge XANES spectra of different Co standard compounds and Co sorption on carbon dots (CoCl2/CDs).
K-edge XANES spectra of different Cu standard compounds and Cu sorption on carbon dots (CuCl2/CDs).
FTIR spectra of CDs and CoCl2/CDs.
Fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs at λem = 432.5 nm and emission spectrum of CDs at λex = 312 nm.
Relative fluorescence responses at 432.5 nm (λex = 312 nm) of the CDs toward different ions.
The relationship between Io/I and the concentration of Co2+ from 1.7 to 167.7 μM (λem = 432.5 nm, λex = 312 nm).
K-edge energies and energy shifts of Cr, Co, and Cu in various compounds.
Cr and Cu percentages of the LCF components representing K2Cr2O7/CDs and CuCl2/CDs samples.
Comparison of the limit of detection and linear range for different Co2+ methods.

head.ce:title: X-ray absorption spectroscopy examination of Cr, Co, and Cu binding on fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Srinakharinwirot University, Bangkok, 10110, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Srinakharinwirot University, Bangkok, 10110, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Synchrotron Light Research Institute, Nakhon Ratchasima, 30000, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Synchrotron Light Research Institute, Nakhon Ratchasima, 30000, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from tartaric acid and triethylenetetramine showed potential for the detection of Cr6+, Co2+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solution, with the most effective sensing being based on fluorescence quenching for Co2+. A good linear relationship between the concentration of Co2+ and the relative fluorescent intensity was obtained at 1.7–167.7 μM (R2 = 0.9993) with a limit of detection at 0.94 μM. X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) was used to examine the absorbed transition metal ions on the CDs. Measurements were carried out at Beamline 8, Synchrotron Light Research Institute, Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand. Cr, Co, and Cu K-edges were measured in various standard compounds containing different oxidation states. An energy shift of 12.49 eV in the absorption of the Co K-edge was observed from elemental Co0 to Co2+, 3+, while shifts of 17.96 eV and 5.45 eV were observed for elemental Cr0 to Cr6+ and elemental Cu0 to Cu2+, respectively. A small decrease in edge energy of the Cr K-edge XAS spectra compared to standard compounds with the LCF results suggested two forms of Cr6+ found in Cr sorption on carbon dots while a mix of Cu1+ and Cu2+ was found in Cu sorption on carbon dots. Interestingly, an increase in edge energy of Co K-edge was observed for Co sorption on carbon dots compared to +2 oxidation state compounds, suggesting that metal species transform to a higher positive charge during binding to CDs. Cobalt species might participate in the formation of a chemical bond with functional groups existing on the surface of CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra confirmed the results from XAS.

ce:section.ce:para: Transition metals such as cobalt, copper, and chromium are natural earth elements found in many different forms throughout the environment after their release from industries involved in electroplating, mining, and paint and chemical production. They are also beneficial for health such as cobalt in vitamin B12, or the role-playing of copper in making red blood cells and maintaining nerve cells. However, excessive intake of cobalt and chromium can cause harmful side effects such as severe cardiovascular or respiratory conditions and cancer (Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), 2019a, b, c). Menkes disease is explained by a deficiency of copper and Wilson disease is characterized by excessive copper content in the body. According to the ATSDR, the oral Minimal Risk Levels (MRLs) for daily exposure to cobalt, copper, and chromium are 0.01, 0.1, and 0.005 mg metal/kg/day, respectively. Simple and effective approaches for the sensitive and selective detection of metal ions are required. Carbon dots are nanomaterials that possess attractive properties including water solubility, chemical inertness, a large specific surface area, biocompatibility, and abundant edge sites for functionalization (Dong et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2013; Barman et al., 2014; Wang and Hu, 2014; Zang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Suzuki et al., 2017). They can also be considered as a class of fluorescent nano-objects with properties attributed to emissions from the carbon-core structure and surface electronic transitions. Fluorescent CDs can be achieved at different emission colors mainly by changing the carbon sources, controlling carbon aggregation, and changing the type of interface groups (So et al., 2017). Functionalized carbon dots can suit many applications including those requiring good selectivity and sensitivity toward various metal ions (Yang et al., 2013; Campos et al., 2016; Suzuki et al., 2017). The detection of ions can be achieved using spectrophotometric methods. Various studies reported different CD designs with specific sensing for the detection of metal ions (Dong et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013; Barman et al., 2014; Gogoi et al., 2015).

X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) is a sensitive technique that can be used to determine metal binding on a variety of materials, and can identify the electronic structure of metals in the chemical environments of the monitored atoms. A change in the absorption edge of metals is well known as a means to identify oxidation states with applications in many fields (Berry and O'Neill, 2004; Hutson et al., 2007; Kravtsova et al., In press; Kamyshova et al.,2019; In press; Shapovalov et al., In press). XAS was utilized to examine C K-edge CDs with various modified surfaces (Ren et al., 2019).

In this study, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from tartaric acid and triethylenetetramine via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The CDs were used as a probe for the detection of Cr6+, Co2+, and Cu2+ in aqueous solution based on fluorescence quenching. The functional groups of the synthesized CDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. XAS was conducted to inspect the metal ions sorption on the CDs. The structure analysis revealed that CDs show superior metal ion chelation with O- and N-donor atoms.

Tartaric acid, triethylenetetramine, NaCl, CaCl2, KNO3, Na2SO4, CdCl2, Na2C2O4, KSCN, K2Cr2O7, HgCl2, NiCl2.6H2O, CuSO4.5H2O, CoCl2.6H2O, Cr2O3, CrO3, CoCl2, Co3O4, CoO, CuCl, and CuCl2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Cr foil, Co foil, and Cu foil were obtained from Exafs Materials, USA. All chemicals were analytical grade reagents and were used as supplied.

Carbon dots were synthesized using a microwave digestion system in Teflon vessels (CEM/MARS 6-CEM Corporation). Fluorescence and UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded using a FP-8300 JASCO spectrofluorometer and V-750 JASCO spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on a Spectrum Two PerkinElmer FTIR spectrometer. X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectra were obtained from an X-ray absorption spectrometer at Synchrotron Light Research Institute, Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Tartaric acid (6.8 mmol) and triethylenetetramine (13.6 mmol) with 4.00 mL DI water were mixed and transferred to a Teflon vessel and then placed in a microwave oven, heated at a rate of 10 °C/min, and kept at 500W for 3 min. The thick yellow viscous liquid obtained was diluted 50-fold with DI water to make it thinner and to produce more liquid for further use. The UV–Visible spectrum and fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs were assessed using a V-750 JASCO spectrophotometer and a FP-8300 JASCO spectrofluorometer, respectively. The FTIR spectra of dry sample CDs were taken on a Spectrum Two PerkinElmer FTIR spectrometer.

In the standard experiment, 5.00 mL of diluted CD solution was added to 1.00 mL of different concentrations of Co2+ solutions. The UV–Visible spectrum was measured by V-750 JASCO spectrophotometer and the fluorescence spectrum of the solution was measured by FP-8300 JASCO spectrofluorometer at an excitation wavelength of 312 nm. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

The Co2+ selectivity was determined by adding other metal ion solutions (NaCl, CaCl2, KNO3, Na2SO4, CdCl2, Na2C2O4, KSCN, K2Cr2O7, HgCl2, NiCl2, CuSO4, and CoCl2) and following similar procedures to Co2+.

The Cr, Co, and Cu K-edge XANES spectra were collected in transmission for reference samples and fluorescence modes for carbon dot sorbent samples at room temperature using a Ge (220) double crystal monochromator. Measurements were carried out at Beamline 8, Synchrotron Light Research Institute (Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand), with a storage ring running at 1.2 GeV and beam current of 50–150 Ma (Klysubun et al., 2012; Klysubun et al., In press). The Cr K-edge (5989 eV) was investigated for different Cr compounds including Cr foil, Cr2O3, CrO3, and K2Cr2O7 with Cr present as 0, +3, +6 and + 6 oxidation states. For Co K-edge (7709 eV), measurements were investigated for different Co compounds including Co foil, CoCl2, CoO, and Co3O4 with Co present as 0, +2, +2 and a mix of +2 and + 3 oxidation states. Finally, Cu K-edge (8979 eV) was investigated in different Cu compounds such as Cu foil, CuCl, and CuCl2, with Cu present as 0, +1 and + 2 oxidation states. The reference samples were ground and homogeneously spread on polymide tape (Lammar Inc., USA) and covered with the same tape for collection of the spectra with the transmission mode. The Cr sorption on carbon dots or K2Cr2O7/CDs was prepared by mixing an aqueous metal ion solution of 2.5 × 10−3 M K2Cr2O7 with 2% v/v CD aqueous solution in the ratio 5:5. For preparation of Co sorption on carbon dots (CoCl2/CDs) and Cu sorption on carbon dots (CuCl2/CDs), 2.5 × 10−3 M K2Cr2O7 aqueous solution was replaced with 2.5 × 10−3 M CoCl2 and 2.5 × 10−3 M CuCl2, respectively. The liquid samples were loaded into thin Teflon sample holders with covered cell windows on both sides by polymide tape. The K-edge XANES spectra of the carbon dot sorbent samples were measured in fluorescence mode with a 13-element Ge detector.

XANES spectra were divided into three regions with the following relative energy ranges and energy steps: −100 to −20 eV (5 eV step), −20 to 80 eV (0.3 eV step), and 80–200 eV (5 eV step). The dwell time for each step across all energy regions of reference samples was 1 s and each region of the carbon dot sorbent samples recorded 5, 10, and 5 s. Two to eight scans were collected from each sample. The monochromatic X-ray beam sizes on each sample were 13 mm (width) x 2 mm (height) and 17 mm (width) x 2 mm (height) for the transmission and fluorescence modes, respectively. Pre-edge and post-edge background subtraction, followed by a normalization procedure for all XANES spectra, was achieved using the ATHENA program (Ravel and Newville, 2005). Linear combination fitting (LCF) was performed without a priori assumptions about the number of components in the mixtures by using all combinations of reference samples. The fitting was performed across the relative energy range of −20 to 80 eV. The goodness of fit was judged by the R factor values.

Figs. 1–3
show the K-edge XANES sample spectra for various Cr, Co, and Cu standard compounds. The spectra were plotted as peak intensity vs. energy position. The K-edge energy, as K-edge shifts for Cr, Cu, and Co was presented in standard compounds and metal sorption on CDs samples as shown in Table 1 . Each metal K-edge shift in a compound was defined as the difference in edge energy with respect to the metal foil. By comparing the edge region for XANES spectra with the standard compounds, the oxidation states of metal sorption on CD samples were determined.

The Cr K-edge XAS spectra were reported for different Cr standard compounds. Cr sorption on the carbon dots is shown in Fig. 1. An energy shift of 17.96 eV in the absorption Cr K-edge was observed from elemental Cr0 to Cr6+ (Table 1). A similar energy shift was found for Cr sorption on carbon dots (K2Cr2O7/CDs) with respect to Cr6+ in K2Cr2O7 and CrO3 standard compounds. Moreover, the pre-edge region revealed tetragonally coordinated Cr2O7 2− sorption on the CDs. Table 2 shows the experimental data fitted by the best combination of LCF components where the Cr percentage found in K2Cr2O7/CDs is reported. K2Cr2O7 and CrO3 are the primary (~83.5 Cr%) and secondary (~16.5 Cr%) forms in samples. The R-factor value is 0.0062, indicating the high quality of the fits. Since the pH value of prepared K2Cr2O7/CDs solution was in the acidic range, the primary form may be due to the electrostatic attraction between Cr2O7 2− and the positively charged CDs surface. The secondary form is likely due to the interaction of Cr6+ with amino groups (NH2) and carboxyl groups (COO−) on the CDs surface (Gorzin and Abadi, 2018). CrO3 forms from dichromate (Cr2O7 2−) via the loss of the chromate ion (CrO4 2−) which is a protonated form of Cr2O7 2− and can also be formed from the addition of H2O to CrO3 followed by deprotonation as shown below.
Cr2O7 2− + H2O ⇌ 2HCrO4 −
2HCrO4 − + H+ ⇌ H2CrO4
H2CrO4 ⇌ CrO3 + H2O

The Co K-edge XANES spectra are shown in Fig. 2. An energy shift of 12.49 eV was observed for elemental Co0 to Co2+, 3+ (Table 1). An energy shift of the Co K-edge for Co sorption on carbon dots (CoCl2/CDs) was observed at 13.38 eV. This shift was larger than the energy shift of the Co K-edge for CoCl2 compounds which was 10.43 eV. The shift of 2.95 eV in the edge reflection confirmed the oxidation-state change for Co. Results determined that the oxidation state of Co in the CoCl2/CDs sample was above +2. This may be ascribed to the formation of covalent bonding of Co with CDs through their surface groups (-NH2 and –COOH) which were observed from the FTIR spectra (Roy et al., 2015). However, the XANES fingerprint of CoCl2/CDs is not similar to the Co reference samples. The difference in shape of the Co K-edge spectra of the sorption Co ions (CoCl2/CDs) compared with the free Co (CoCl2) is observed. It indicated that the electronic structures of the sorption Co are perturbed. This may reflect the occurrence of binding to several different sites on the surface complex formation of CDs involving coordination number, symmetry, and the nature of the Co–N or Co–O bond.

The Cu K-edge XANES spectra of various compounds are represented in Fig. 3. An energy shift of 5.45 eV was observed in the absorption Cu K-edge in Cu compounds with respect to elemental Cu. A small decrease in edge energy of the Cu K-edge XANES spectra for Cu sorption on CDs (CuCl2/CDs) was observed. The Cu K-edge of the CuCl2/CDs spectrum showed that the average formal valence of Cu was Cu1+/Cu2+. For the LCF result, the Cu K-edge data clearly indicated that CuCl is the primary form in the CuCl2/CDs sample while the secondary form is CuCl2. The significant change observed as the primary form of Cu bound to the CDs implied the occurrence of electron transfer sp2-carbon at the CDs to the bound ions. The 3 d9 in Cu2+ has been changed to 3 d10 in Cu1+ which is more stable (Mintz et al., 2018).

The FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs mixed with CoCl2 are shown in Fig. 4 . The FTIR spectrum of CDs revealed the existence of –COOH and –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H (2800-3300 cm−1), CO stretching (1719 cm−1), CC stretching (1575 cm−1), C–H bending (1418 cm−1), C–N stretching (1356 cm−1), and C–O stretching (1124 cm−1). The substantial decrease in intensity along with the broadness of N–H vibrations was attributed to the shift to low wave numbers of CO stretching vibrations in the CoCl2/CDs spectrum, indicating the coordination of Co ions with amino (-NH2) and carboxyl (COO−) functional groups on the surface of CDs.

The hydrothermal-assisted microwave system provided appreciable CDs. The carbon dots solution was yellow in color and produced blue fluorescence after exposure to UV light. The UV–Visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 , respectively. The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CDs exhibited a peak at 273 nm resulting from the π-π* transition (CC) of aromatic sp2 domains and the absorption at 361 nm corresponding to the n-π* transition (CO) of carbonyl groups contributing to the CD surfaces (Campos et al., 2016). The CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence emission at 432.5 nm under excitation of 312 nm.

The effects of different cations and anions on the fluorescence responses of CDs were studied. Ions at a concentration of 167.7 μM were added into a CD solution with excitation of 312 nm, as shown in Fig. 7 . Cu2+ and Cr2O7 2− ions reduced fluorescence intensity at 432.5 nm, while Co2+ showed strong fluorescence quenching with slight changes for the other ions. Co2+ provided the highest selectivity toward fluorescence quenching of CDs over other common ions. FTIR spectroscopy and XAS results provided information according to the complexation via coordination interaction of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs with Co2+ ions, resulting in fluorescence quenching (Zhang et al., 2013).

The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 432.5 nm decreased gradually with an increasing Co2+ concentration. Fig. 8 shows good linear correlation (y = 0.0020x + 1.1004) over the range of 1.7–167.7 μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9993. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.94 μM (LOD = 3σ/slope). Synthesized CDs showed potential for Co2+ detection with rapid and simple performance.

Table 3 compares the detection limit and linearity concentration range of the proposed sensor with those reported for different methods using nanoprobes of Co2+ detection (Gore et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012; Yao et al., 2010; Jing et al., 2019). The CDs probed here showed a lower LOD than the colorimetry method and also gave a wider linear range than previously reported similar methods.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from tartaric acid and triethylenetetramine via a quick and simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The resulting CDs emitting blue fluorescence were applied to selectively detect Co2+ on the basis of significant fluorescence quenching. The LOD was calculated as 0.94 μM with a linear range of 1.7–167.7 μM. Cr, Co, and Cu K-edge XANES spectra were used to examine metal ions sorption on the synthesized carbon dot interfaces. Cr and Cu were observed in two forms during binding to the CDs. In the case of Co, the edge energy shifted to a higher value for Co sorption on CDs compared to the standard compound. This result suggested that the metal species transformed to a higher positive charge during binding to the CDs. This change was attributed to the participation of Co in the formation of a chemical bond with the functional groups existing on the surface of the CDs.

Budsaya Moonsuang: Data curation. Penphitcha Amonpattaratkit: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Ngamjit Praingam: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Pornpimol Prayongpan: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported through the Faculty of Science, Srinakharinwirot University, Thailand under contract number 262/2561, 263/2561 and 264/2561. The authors thank for SRLI for the allocation of synchrotron radiation beamtime. Assistance provided by beamline 8 (BL8) staff members of the synchrotron facility is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radphyschem.2020.108751.
